mild: I try not to overthink it. Some things just happen.
It bothers me more than I'd like to admit.
I've been thinking about it all week. Can't seem to let it go.
Honestly, I'm just tired of hearing about it.
It's one of those things where you just have to wait and see.
I keep telling myself it's not my problem. Hasn't worked yet.
I noticed it before anyone else did. Nobody listened, of course.
It makes me want to do something, but I don't know what.
I'm choosing to be optimistic. Somebody has to.
If I dwell on it, I'll go in circles. So I don't.
I try not to think about it, but it's hard not to.
I've heard enough opinions to last a lifetime. Still forming my own.
It's the kind of thing I'd rather not have an opinion about, but here I am.
I mentioned it to a friend. They told me not to worry. That didn't help.
I keep going back and forth. Some days it matters, some days it doesn't.
I'd rather focus on what I can control. Which, admittedly, isn't much.
I've decided not to let it bother me. Ask me again tomorrow and I might have changed my mind.
It's in the back of my mind every morning. Not the front: just the back. That's something.
I think about it when things are quiet. Which is exactly when I don't want to.
I asked someone smarter than me what they thought. They said the same thing I was thinking.
Part of me finds it interesting. The rest of me wishes it wasn't happening.
I don't lose sleep over it. Not much, anyway.
It crossed my mind more than once today. I stopped counting after that.
I'm not the worrying type, but I'll admit it's on my radar.
I've made my peace with not knowing the whole picture.
Some things you can fix. Some things you just learn to live with. This feels like the second kind.
I try to take it day by day. That's about all I can manage.
I have a feeling about it, but I couldn't put it into words if I tried.
I'd be lying if I said I hadn't thought about it. But I'm not losing sleep.
It's like a splinter: small enough to ignore, but I know it's there.
I figure if it was really that bad, someone would've done something by now.
I'm staying out of it. That's my policy, and I'm sticking to it.
It nags at me in odd moments. While I'm working, while I'm eating.
I keep expecting it to resolve. It keeps not resolving.
Honestly? I'm more curious than worried. Though the line between those is thin.
I mentioned my thoughts to someone and they laughed. So now I keep them to myself.
It comes and goes. Some weeks I barely think about it. Others it's all I think about.
I wish I had a stronger opinion. Everything just feels uncertain.
I'm treating it the way I treat bad weather: wait it out and stay dry.
I've caught myself staring out the window thinking about it. Not a good use of my time.
It makes me a little uneasy, but not enough to change my routine.
I think most people feel the way I do: mildly concerned and mostly powerless.
I'd feel better if I understood it. Understanding usually helps.
I don't talk about it much. Not because I don't care: because talking doesn't change it.
I have my moments of concern. They pass. Then they come back.
If someone asked me how I felt about it a month ago, I'd have shrugged. Not anymore.
It's one of those quiet worries that doesn't demand attention but never quite leaves.
I try to be practical about these things. Feelings don't fix problems.
I noticed my hands were tense this morning for no reason. Then I remembered.
I told myself I wouldn't get involved. I told myself that last time too.
I'm not angry about it. Just... aware of it. Constantly.
I keep wanting to ask other people how they feel, just to see if I'm being reasonable.
It's like waiting for rain. You can feel it coming, but you don't know when.
I've gotten good at not thinking about things. This one's testing that skill.
I wouldn't say it keeps me up at night. But it's there when I wake up.
My instinct says it's fine. My instinct has been wrong before.
I've stopped checking for updates. Whatever happens will happen whether I'm watching or not.
It feels like something I should care about more than I do. Or maybe less. I can't tell.
I mentioned it to the family and they told me I was making a mountain out of a molehill. Maybe they're right.
I go about my day and then it hits me at odd moments. Like remembering a dream.
I don't have the energy to worry about it properly. So I worry about it half-heartedly.
It's not fear, exactly. More like a low hum of awareness that won't go away.
I catch myself planning around it. As if I've already accepted it'll get worse.
I'm keeping my opinions to myself for once. There's wisdom in that, I think.
I've been in a strange mood all week and I'm only now realizing why.
I figure I'll deal with it when I have to. Not a moment before.
I keep reminding myself that I've gotten through things before. That helps, some.
It doesn't scare me. It just makes me thoughtful. More thoughtful than I usually am.
I sometimes wonder if I'm the only one who feels this way. I doubt it, though.
It hasn't changed my plans. Just the way I feel about them.
If it turns out to be nothing, I'll laugh about how much thought I gave it.
I've noticed I'm being nicer to people lately. I think it's related.
I woke up thinking about it this morning. That was a first.
I've learned to carry a little worry without letting it slow me down.
Some days I think I'm overreacting. Other days I think I'm underreacting. No middle ground.
My feelings on it depend entirely on who I last spoke to about it.
It's taught me something, I think. I just don't know what yet.
I'd rather know than not know. Even if knowing is uncomfortable.
The uncertainty is worse than the thing itself. If I just knew, I could deal.
I keep telling myself it doesn't matter. The telling itself proves it does.
It's added a weight to my days. Small, but noticeable.
Honestly, I'm just glad someone else brought it up. I thought I was the only one thinking about it.
I've been quieter than usual. My family's noticed.
I think about it the way you think about weather: always there, mostly background.
I don't have answers. I barely have the right questions.
It's one more thing on a long list of things I can't do anything about.
I'm neither worried nor unbothered. I'm just... present with it.
I catch myself sighing more. Not dramatically: just small, tired sighs.
It's strange how something can matter so much and so little at the same time.
I've developed a habit of pausing before I leave the house. Just to think. Then I go.
I keep expecting to feel more strongly about it. The middling concern is almost worse.
I'm saving my worrying for when it's useful. Which might be soon.
It sits in my thoughts the way a stone sits in a shoe. Not painful: just there.
I sometimes forget about it entirely, and then the forgetting feels guilty.
I've accepted that I don't know how I feel about it. That's a feeling in itself.
I told myself I'd stop thinking about it after a week. The week passed.
Mostly I feel steady. But steadiness takes more effort than it used to.
I notice it most when things are quiet. Silence gives worry room to stretch.
It hasn't ruined my days. But it's flavored them.
I'd say I'm cautiously indifferent. If that's even a thing.
I'm handling it. Not gracefully, but I'm handling it.
I've noticed I pay more attention to small things now. The way the light changes. The sounds at night.
I asked around and everyone gave me a different answer. That was oddly comforting.
It's the kind of thing that makes you pause at the door before going out. Just for a moment.
I've decided worrying about it is a waste of perfectly good daylight.
My neighbor mentioned it and I pretended I hadn't been thinking the same thing.
Some things settle on their own if you let them. I'm hoping this is one of those.
I find myself looking out the window more than I used to. Not for anything in particular.
It's funny how something so small can take up so much space in your head.
I mentioned it at dinner and the whole table went quiet. That told me plenty.
I've been walking a different route lately. Not consciously: I just noticed I was doing it.
The more I think about it, the less I know what to think. So I keep busy.
It reminds me of something my mother used to say, but I can't remember the exact words.
I keep waiting for someone else to bring it up first. Nobody does.
I'm treating it like weather. You don't control it. You dress for it.
It put a wrinkle in my week that I wasn't expecting. Not a crisis: just a wrinkle.
I've started paying attention to things I used to ignore. Not sure if that's progress or paranoia.
I talked to a friend about it and we both agreed we didn't know what to do. That was somehow the best conversation I'd had all week.
I've been sleeping fine, which surprises me. The days are harder than the nights.
It's given me a mild appreciation for how little I actually understand about anything.
I keep catching myself in half-thoughts about it. Never a complete idea: just fragments.
If nothing else, it's given me something to talk about. Not that I enjoy talking about it.
I've learned to sit with the discomfort. Turns out discomfort is patient.
It's not urgent. It's just persistent. Like a dripping tap in another room.
I shared my thoughts with someone and they said 'huh' and changed the subject. Fair enough.
I've found that doing something useful helps more than sitting and thinking. The thinking doesn't produce answers.
My feelings about it change depending on how much sleep I've gotten. That seems important.
It sits somewhere between an annoyance and a concern. There should be a word for that.
I keep noticing patterns that probably aren't patterns. The mind fills in what it wants to see.
I'd feel better about it if someone in charge seemed to have a plan. Or even an opinion.
It hasn't changed what I do each day. Just what I think about while I'm doing it.
I've become a better listener since it started. Not by choice: I just want to hear what others think.
I realized I've been clenching my jaw during the day. I made myself stop. Then I caught myself doing it again.
I keep a list in my head of things I'm grateful for. It's longer than I expected.
I asked the elder about it and she said she'd seen worse. That didn't help as much as she thought it would.
I've noticed I'm funnier lately. Stress does that to some people. I'm apparently one of them.
It's the waiting that wears on me. If something would just happen, at least I'd know.
I keep thinking I should have an opinion by now. The fact that I don't feels like an opinion in itself.
I mentioned it in passing and someone grabbed my arm and asked what I knew. I knew exactly as much as they did.
It's the kind of thing I'll either remember for years or forget by next month. I genuinely can't tell which.
intense: Honestly? It rattled me. I haven't slept right since.
I've seen a lot in my years, and this unsettles me.
Part of me wants to know more. The smarter part wants to know less.
Something about it just sits heavy. I don't have a better way to say it.
I'm not scared, exactly. More like aware of how small I am.
That thought has stayed with me since. I can't shake it.
I laughed when they first told me. I'm not laughing now.
Some mornings I wake up and forget about it for a moment. Those are good mornings.
I've made my peace with not understanding it. Doesn't mean I'm at peace.
It's strange: the worst part isn't the danger. It's the not knowing.
I'd be lying if I said I wasn't curious. Terrified, but curious.
My grandmother would've had a prayer for this. I wish I remembered it.
I catch myself staring into the distance now. Just watching. Waiting for nothing.
I won't pretend I'm brave. I'm still here, though. That counts for something.
There's a knot in my chest that won't go away. I've learned to work around it.
You get used to the unease. That's maybe the most unsettling part of all.
It changed how I see things. Not dramatically, but enough to notice.
If I dwell on it, I'll go mad. So I keep busy. Very, very busy.
I've started keeping a journal. Figured someone should write it down, just in case.
Something broke inside when I heard. Not loudly: more like a hinge giving way.
The fear comes in waves. I've learned to ride them instead of fighting.
I haven't told anyone how much it affects me. People rely on me to be steady.
I dreamed about it last night. First time something from waking life followed me there.
I've rearranged my priorities since. The things that used to matter seem smaller now.
I keep looking at the people around me and wondering if they feel it too.
My hands trembled when I first heard. They've since stopped. Mostly.
I've thought about leaving. Seriously thought about it. I'm still thinking.
There's a before and an after now. I can feel the line between them.
I used to sleep with the window open. I don't anymore.
I pray now. I didn't use to. Make of that what you will.
It's humbling. Whatever else I thought I understood about the world, I was wrong about some of it.
I've started doing things I kept putting off. Just in case.
The worst part is pretending everything is normal when I know it isn't.
I hold my children a little tighter now. They've noticed.
I've been shorter with people than I should be. The worry comes out as impatience.
I find myself memorizing things: faces, the way light falls, the sound of the market. As if I might lose them.
I didn't think I was the type to be shaken. Turns out I was just the type who hadn't been shaken yet.
I've made contingency plans. I haven't told anyone, but I know which road I'd take.
The weight of it is physical. I carry it in my shoulders, my jaw, my fists.
I keep replaying what I know in my head, looking for the thing I missed. There has to be something I missed.
I tried to explain it to someone and my voice cracked. I pretended it was the cold.
It's not fear I feel: it's grief. For the way things were. For the certainty I used to have.
I was angry at first. The anger has faded. What replaced it is harder to name.
I caught myself counting our supplies yesterday. Not because I needed to: because I needed the comfort of a number.
I've stopped making long-term plans. That frightens me more than the thing itself.
There's a version of me from a month ago who would not recognize how I feel now.
I find peace in routine. The doing keeps the thinking at bay. When I stop working, it all rushes back.
I told someone I was fine. The lie came so easily it scared me.
I've become careful with words. Saying the wrong thing feels riskier now.
Something in me shifted, like a compass finding a new north. I don't know what I'm pointed at anymore.
I watch the sunset differently now. Like it's something I earned by making it through the day.
The silence at night used to be peaceful. Now it's just empty.
I'm exhausted in a way that sleep doesn't fix.
I've thought about it from every angle. None of them are comforting.
My appetite's been off. My family thinks I'm sick. I let them think that. It's simpler.
I keep looking for signs that things are returning to normal. I haven't found any.
There are things I want to say to people that I can't bring myself to say. Not yet.
I feel older than I did a season ago. Not wiser: just older.
I've caught myself flinching at ordinary sounds. A door. A bird. My own name.
I keep a knife closer than I used to. I don't know who I think I'll use it against.
The fear is manageable. What isn't manageable is the guilt of not doing more.
I wrote a letter to my family elsewhere. Haven't sent it. Writing it was the point.
I've realized I don't know what I'd do if it got worse. That lack of plan haunts me.
I've been kinder to people since. Softer. Fear makes some people mean. It made me gentle.
I think about the people who've already left and wonder if they were the smart ones.
It's clarifying, in a terrible way. I know exactly what matters to me now.
I've stopped arguing with anyone about anything. Life feels too short for it suddenly.
The courage I show during the day costs me at night. I pay for it in sleeplessness.
I find myself touching walls, trees, solid things. As if checking that the world is still real.
I trust my instincts more than I trust anyone's reassurances right now.
It's given me a strange respect for the people who went through worse and came out the other side.
I feel like I'm waiting for something, but I don't know what. The waiting is the hardest part.
I used to think I was resilient. Now I think I was just untested.
I look at the stars differently. Less pretty, more vast. The kind of vast that diminishes you.
I haven't cried about it. I'm not sure if that's strength or numbness.
I spend more time than I should standing at the edge of the settlement, just looking outward.
I've learned things about myself I didn't want to know. How quickly I considered running. How seriously.
The person I talk to most about it is myself. And even that conversation goes in circles.
I stay up later than I should. Not doing anything: just being awake. As if sleep is giving up.
It sits behind every conversation I have. Like a third person in the room.
I've noticed beauty more sharply since. A terrible trade, but there it is.
I keep checking on people I care about. More than they need. But I need it.
I've run through worst-case scenarios so many times they've lost their edge. That worries me.
I'm holding together for the people around me. If I were alone, I don't know.
Something about it stripped away the pretense. I'm not who I thought I was. Maybe that's alright.
I'm still standing. Some days that's the accomplishment.
There are moments when the world looks exactly the same as before, and those moments are the cruelest.
I talk about it calmly because I've practiced. The calm is performance.
I've become very aware of exits. Doors, roads, paths through the trees. I map them without thinking.
The hardest part isn't the fear. It's the loneliness of feeling it while everyone pretends they don't.
I've started measuring time differently. Before and after. Then and now.
I think I understand why the old stories always have survivors who seem changed. Because you are.
I've been braver than I thought possible. I've also been more afraid. Turns out those aren't opposites.
I hold onto small routines like ropes in a current. They keep me from drifting.
The thought that I might look back on this and laugh seems impossible right now.
I've found out what my breaking point isn't. I haven't found out what it is yet.
Something fundamental shifted. Like the ground is the same but my feet don't trust it.
I tell myself this will pass. The telling is an act of will, not belief.
I've noticed I speak more quietly now. As if volume itself is a risk.
There's a watchfulness in me that wasn't there before. I don't think it's leaving.
I keep one bag packed. I tell myself it's practical. It's not: it's fear with a handle.
I've never been more grateful for ordinary days. A boring day is a gift now.
Every time I walk to the well, I look around more carefully than I should need to.
It's changed me. I'm not ready to say how, but it has.
I think about what I would tell my younger self. I wouldn't tell them anything. Let them have the peace.
The person I was before this seems like a stranger. Comfortable. Unwary. I miss that person.
I've found a kind of resolve I didn't know I had. It's colder than I expected.
I keep thinking: if not me, who? And if not now, when? And then I don't move.
The fear has become a kind of companion. Not welcome: just present.
I've stopped asking if it will be okay. I've started asking what I'll do if it isn't.
I carry it with me the way you carry a bruise. Carefully. Aware of every bump.
I've never wanted to be wrong about something so badly in my life.
Somewhere between the worry and the work, I found something I didn't expect: a strange, brittle calm.
If I make it through this, I'll be different. Not better or worse: just different. I can feel it happening.
I wake in the dark sometimes and listen. Just listen. As if the night might tell me something.
I've discovered that courage and fear feel exactly the same. The only difference is what you do next.
There are things I never said to people I care about. That oversight haunts me now.
The uncertainty is a living thing. It breathes with me. It walks beside me.
I don't know what's coming. But I know I'll face it standing. That's all I've got.
I've started locking my door at night. I never used to lock my door.
My voice shakes when I talk about it. I try to cover it with coughing.
I've been giving things away. Small things. As if lightening my load matters.
There's a heaviness behind my eyes that I can't blink away. It's been there for weeks.
I watched a child playing yesterday and felt a grief I couldn't explain.
I've started telling people things I appreciate about them. They look at me strangely.
The silence between conversations feels louder than it used to. As if it's full of something waiting.
I work until my hands ache because stopping means thinking, and thinking leads nowhere good.
I've memorized the fastest way to every exit in the settlement. I didn't set out to: it just happened.
There's a calm in me that frightens me. It feels like resignation wearing a mask.
I've been eating less. Not by choice: the appetite just isn't there.
I tried to explain it to someone once. Halfway through, I realized I was explaining it to myself.
The ordinary sounds of the settlement: carts, voices, hammering. They ground me. When they stop, I drift.
I've noticed my hands are steadier than they should be. Some part of me has accepted what the rest hasn't.
I catch myself standing at the well, bucket in hand, staring at nothing. People have started walking around me.
There's a conversation happening in my head that never concludes. It just circles.
I dream about wide open spaces now. Running through them. I think my mind is rehearsing.
I've become fiercely protective of my routine. Any change, no matter how small, feels like a threat.
Some mornings I stand in the doorway and count the people I can see. I don't know why. The number never means anything.
I said goodbye to someone yesterday in a way that made them ask if I was alright. I said yes. I wasn't.
I've been kinder to strangers than to people I love. The kindness is easier when there's no history.
The weight isn't constant. It lifts sometimes, and in those moments the world is unbearably beautiful.
I've caught myself bargaining with the silence. If I do this, if I'm careful, maybe nothing will change.
I keep a small stone in my pocket that I found the day before it all started. I hold it when my thoughts race.
There's a crack in my composure that I plaster over every morning. Some days the plaster holds.
I've stopped asking why. The answer doesn't help. I've started asking what next. That helps a little.
I feel like I'm living in two timelines: one where everything is fine, and one where nothing is.
I look at the faces of people who don't seem worried and wonder what they know that I don't. Or what I know that they don't.
My jaw aches in the morning. I've been grinding my teeth in my sleep.
I wrote down everything I know and everything I'm afraid of. The second list was longer.
I've been more patient with difficult people. When you're carrying something heavy, the small irritations seem smaller.
There's a version of this that ends well. I visit it in my mind sometimes, like checking on a friend.
I tell myself that fear is information: it tells you what matters. But it also tells you what you might lose.
I've learned to recognize the moment when worry turns to dread. I try to step back before it crosses.
Some nights I just sit with a candle and breathe. In and out. It's the simplest thing that helps.
I'm tired of being the calm one. Just once, I'd like to be the person someone else reassures.
I measure my days differently now. Not by what I accomplished, but by whether I kept going.
I've found that the people who talk about it the most know the least. The quiet ones are the ones paying attention.
I keep a lantern burning through the night now. The dark feels different than it used to.
There's a part of me that's already mourning something I haven't lost yet. That's the strangest grief.
I've stopped flinching when people bring it up. Not because I'm braver: because I've heard it enough times that the words have lost their edges.
